concise:
Ayah 1–14 · linked video: 01. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 1-14)

Surah Ash-Shura: Ayahs 1-14

Ayah 1

حمٓ

Ha Meem - these are the mysterious letters that open this surah. Ash-Shura is the final surah in a sequence of four interconnected chapters that began with Az-Zumar. These surahs have been building toward a climax, particularly around the theme of Muslims coming together not merely as individuals, but as a unified collective devoted to serving Allah’s ﷻ religion.

The mystery of these opening letters is addressed immediately in the following ayah, where Allah ﷻ essentially tells us: this is how revelation works. You must submit to it. There are wisdoms you understand and wisdoms that remain beyond your grasp.

Ayah 2

عٓسٓقٓ

Ayn Seen Qaf - another set of mysterious letters unique to the Quran. These letters stand as a testament to the divine nature of this Book, reminding us that complete comprehension of Allah’s ﷻ wisdom is not required for sincere submission.

Ayah 3

كَذَٰلِكَ يُوحِىٓ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

Thus does He reveal to you, and to those before you - Allah ﷻ, the Exalted in Might, the Wise. This ayah explains the purpose of the mysterious letters: كَذَٰلِكَ - “That is how We have revealed to you.” The message is clear: you must submit to the divine wisdom, whether you fully grasp it or not.

Allah ﷻ describes Himself here as Al-Aziz Al-Hakeem - the One with ultimate authority to reveal what He wills, and the Owner of perfect wisdom. He may expose certain dimensions of knowledge while keeping others veiled. This is His ﷻ divine prerogative as the source of all revelation, both to Muhammad ﷺ and to all the messengers who came before him.

Ayah 4

لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and He is the Most High, the Most Great. This ayah establishes Allah’s ﷻ complete ownership and authority. Everything in existence - from the highest heavens to the depths of the earth - belongs to Him ﷻ alone. He is Al-Aliyy Al-Azeem, ultimately elevated above all creation and infinitely magnificent.

Ayah 5

تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِن فَوْقِهِنَّ ۚ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

The heavens almost rupture from above them, and the angels glorify the praises of their Lord and ask forgiveness for those on earth. Unquestionably, it is Allah ﷻ who is the Forgiving, the Merciful.

The imagery here is staggering: the skies are on the verge of tearing open from above themselves. Meanwhile, the angels continuously declare the perfection of their Master through praise and persistently seek forgiveness on behalf of humanity dwelling on earth. This paints a powerful picture of the cosmic reality - the heavens trembling with the weight of divine majesty while the angels intercede for us.

The ayah concludes with emphatic reassurance: أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ - “No doubt, Allah ﷻ, in fact He is the extremely Forgiving, the always Merciful.” Despite our shortcomings, His ﷻ mercy remains constant and His ﷻ forgiveness is ever-accessible.

Ayah 6

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ

And those who take protectors besides Him - Allah ﷻ is Guardian over them, and you are not a manager over them. This ayah addresses those who place their trust in other than Allah ﷻ. The irony is profound: they seek guardians and protectors, yet Allah ﷻ Himself is guarding those very guardians they’ve chosen. Their so-called protectors are themselves under Allah’s ﷻ watchful care.

The Prophet ﷺ is then told directly: وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ - “You are not responsible over their matters.” The word wakeel means someone who manages and disposes of affairs. The message is clear: you cannot force guidance upon them or take care of their spiritual business for them. Your role is to convey the message, not to control hearts.

Ayah 7

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَتُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلْجَمْعِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ فَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلسَّعِيرِ

And thus We have revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it, and warn of the Day of Assembly, about which there is no doubt. A party will be in Paradise and a party in the Blaze.

The phrase أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ ( - “Mother of Cities” - refers to Makkah, which was the social, economic and political capital of the region. Allah ﷻ has consistently sent messengers to central cities, and this pattern continues with the Prophet ﷺ being sent to the heart of Arabia.

The description “Mother of Cities” carries multiple dimensions of meaning. First, it makes perfect sense that the revelation would be in Arabic if it’s being sent to the capital of all Arab territories, especially considering that the Arabs prided themselves on their eloquence and linguistic mastery. Earlier passages addressed accusations that the Prophet ﷺ might be learning from a foreigner; now the emphasis is: why would the Quran be anything other than Arabic if it’s coming to the center of Arabia itself?

Second, there’s a deeper historical significance. If we consider the opinion that the House of Allah was originally built by Adam (AS), then Makkah truly is the mother of all cities - the first city, the site of the first construction ever undertaken by humanity.

Third, there’s a forward-looking dimension: from this point until the end of the world, the capital of the world will be the city that houses Allah’s ﷻ Sacred House. Already, a fifth of humanity recognizes it as such, and this recognition will only continue to grow.

The phrase وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا (and whoever is around it) is deliberately open-ended. Initially, this might mean nearby cities like Madinah, Taif and Yemen. Expand the circle further and it includes Syria, Palestine, Iraq and Persia. Continue expanding and it encompasses Turkey, Europe and the rest of Asia. There is no limit to this expansion - the message is universal.

The ayah then shifts to the Day of Judgment: لِّتُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلْجَمْعِ (that you may warn of the Day of Assembly). Notice that this Day is called the “Day of Gathering,” and that gathering will take place in proximity to this capital city. The connection between Umm al-Qura and the ultimate gathering is no coincidence.

The ayah concludes with stark clarity: لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ (in which there is no doubt whatsoever) - one group will be in Paradise, another in the Blaze.

Ayah 8

وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَآءُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَا لَهُم مِّن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ

And if Allah ﷻ had willed, He could have made them one nation, but He admits whom He wills into His mercy. And the wrongdoers will have neither protector nor helper.

Allah ﷻ could have created all of humanity as a single believing nation. He has that power. However, He enters into His mercy whoever He wants, giving every nation the opportunity to choose. The choice to accept guidance and enter Allah’s ﷻ mercy remains open to all.

The ayah ends with a sobering reality: وَٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَا لَهُم مِّن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ - wrongdoers will find no protective friend and no aid. Despite their attempts to seek protectors other than Allah ﷻ, they will ultimately stand alone.

Ayah 9

أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ وَهُوَ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Or have they taken protectors besides Him? But Allah ﷻ - He is the Protector, and He gives life to the dead, and He is over all things competent.

This ayah poses a rhetorical question: is it really the case that they’ve taken protective guardians besides Allah ﷻ? The answer follows immediately with emphatic clarity: فَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ - “But Allah ﷻ, He in fact is the ultimate Guardian, the ultimate Protector.”

The ayah then establishes Allah’s ﷻ absolute power through two declarations. First, He gives life to the dead - a power that no created being possesses. Second, He is in complete control over all things. These attributes make the pursuit of any other protector utterly futile.

Ayah 10

وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِيهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فَحُكْمُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ

And in anything over which you disagree - its ruling is to be referred to Allah ﷻ. That is Allah ﷻ, my Lord; upon Him I have relied, and to Him I turn back.

This ayah marks a crucial transition. Up until this point, the discussion has centered on metaphysical beliefs - about Allah’s ﷻ power, His mercy, His role as Protector. But now these beliefs transform into something concrete: the rule, the governance, the decision-making capacity should belong to Allah ﷻ.

Whatever disagreements arise among you in any matter, فَحُكْمُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - its final verdict must go back to Allah ﷻ. The real Governor of society needs to be Allah ﷻ Himself.

The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to declare: ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى (That is Allah ﷻ, in fact, my Lord). He has already declared Allah’s ﷻ authority over his own life, but now the people must do the same. What we’re witnessing is the shift from individual submission to collective submission - from personal transformation to societal transformation. Earlier, we discussed how accepting Tawheed requires changing your individual lifestyle. Now we see that at the collective level, an entire society is being called to restructure itself according to divine authority.

Ayah 11

فَاطِرُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ أَزْوَٰجًا ۖ يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ ۚ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ

He is the Originator of the heavens and the earth. He has made for you from yourselves mates, and among the cattle mates; He multiplies you thereby. There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the Hearing, the Seeing.

Allah ﷻ is described as Fatir - the One who originates and perfects creation. He made spouses from among yourselves, and He made spouses among the cattle as well. Through this pairing system, He multiplies and spreads you throughout creation. The word يَذْرَؤُكُمْ captures the idea of dispersing and distributing - like a construction project being built and spread out.

Then comes one of the most significant declarations in the Quran: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌ - “Nothing is even close to being like Him.” This isn’t merely saying “nothing is like Him” - the phrasing emphasizes that nothing is even in the category of resembling Him, nothing approaches equivalence to Him.

The ayah concludes: وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ - “And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing.” Despite His absolute transcendence, He is intimately aware of all that we say and all that we do.

Ayah 12

لَهُۥ مَقَالِيدُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth. He extends provision for whom He wills and restricts it. Indeed, He is of all things Knowing.

The word مَقَالِيدُ ) refers to keys to treasure vaults - Allah ﷻ alone controls access to all the treasures of the heavens and the earth. He expands provisions for whomever He wants and He restricts them.

Notice the grammatical structure at the end of this ayah: إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ. The pronoun “He” (innahu) is so compressed in the text that it emerges as a subtle whisper, yet its meaning is emphatic: “Indeed, He is fully knowledgeable of all things.” The constricted form mirrors the content about restricting provisions, demonstrating the Quran’s linguistic precision.

Ayah 13

۞ شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِۦ نُوحًا وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِۦٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰٓ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ كَبُرَ عَلَى ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۚ ٱللَّهُ يَجْتَبِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ

He has ordained for you of religion what He enjoined upon Nuh and that which We have revealed to you, and what We enjoined upon Ibrahim and Musa and Isa - to establish the religion and not be divided therein. Difficult for those who associate others with Allah ﷻ is that to which you invite them. Allah ﷻ chooses for Himself whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back.

This is one of the weightiest ayahs in the Quran, tracing the continuity of divine guidance across human history. The word شَرَعَ means “He carved out a way” - from the same root as Shariah. In classical Arabic, this word relates to taking a camel to water; the camel must choose to drink on its own. This captures the essence of Shariah: it is a source of life, like water in the desert, but it cannot be forced upon anyone. The choice to accept it must be voluntary.

Allah ﷻ says: He carved out this way for you from the religion - the same way He established with Nuh (AS). The sequence is significant: Nuh (AS) is mentioned first because his message reached all of humanity. After the flood, his children were the only ones left, making him effectively the second father of humanity after Adam (AS). In this sense, Nuh (AS) was a messenger to all of humanity, just as Muhammad ﷺ is the final messenger to all of humanity. The sequence thus highlights the two universal messengers, breaking chronological order to emphasize this connection.

Then the ayah states: وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ - “and that which We have revealed to you.” Notice the shift in language. With Nuh (AS), Allah ﷻ uses مَا (what), which is ambiguous and indefinite. “Whatever we gave to Nuh” - meaning we know some of what he was given, but not everything. The details are not fully preserved.

But with Muhammad ﷺ, Allah ﷻ uses ٱلَّذِى (that which), which is definite and precise. What has been given to the Prophet ﷺ is extremely well documented, more so than any other messenger in history. His life, his teachings, the circumstances of revelation - all preserved with unprecedented accuracy. His revelation becomes specific and exact while the others remain general in our knowledge.

Then the ayah returns to مَا again: “and what We enjoined upon Ibrahim and Musa and Isa.” The sequence matters here too. After mentioning Muhammad ﷺ, the universal messenger to this era, the ayah mentions Ibrahim (AS) - the prophet with whom the Arabs immediately identify. Who else will the Prophet ﷺ be dealing with in Arabia? The Jews and Christians, the People of the Book. So which two prophets are mentioned alongside Ibrahim (AS)? Musa (AS) and Isa (AS) - making it clear that this message encompasses and represents the legacy of all these prophets.

The core message given to all these messengers is beautifully concise: أَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ - “Establish the religion and do not be divided therein.”

The word أَقِيمُوا۟ - “establish” - is different from اسْتَقِيمُوا - “be firm.” We’ve seen istaqeemu before in Surah Fussilat: those who say “Our Lord is Allah” and then remain steadfast. Istiqamah is about you standing firm on your own. But iqamah is about making something else stand - building it up as an institution, as a structure.

When we say “establish the salah,” we don’t just mean perform it personally. We mean make salah exist as an institution in society, as something that stands firm in the community. Similarly, “establish the deen” means to build and construct the religion as a living reality in life - in individuals, in families, in communities.

And this doesn’t happen overnight. Construction takes time. It takes time to establish principles of religion in our lives. It takes time to become truly disciplined about salah. It takes time to train our tongues, to stop backbiting, to control our anger. The same way, it takes time to establish a community, to build a masjid, to create genuine brotherhood, to implement zakah uniformly. These things take time.

But all the messengers were taught: this is the goal. This is what they worked toward. Little by little, day by day, the deen would become like a tree with roots growing deeper and deeper into the earth. And the deeper the roots, the higher the branches grow. You can think of the Makkan period of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission as the roots of the tree - establishing faith, building character, creating a solid foundation. The Madinan period represents the growth of the tree and its branches - the laws, the governance, the full flowering of Islamic civilization. Both elements are essential.

وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ - “And do not be divided therein.” It’s critical to understand the word choice here. The word for division is تَفَرُّق , not اخْتِلَاف . Ikhtilaf means disagreement, which may or may not be problematic. We know that even angels have ikhtilaf - when Allah ﷻ told them He would create Adam (AS), they questioned it, but there was no division among them. Their disagreement didn’t lead to separation.

In matters of deen, we can have ikhtilaf - differences of opinion. There are various positions within fiqh, different schools of thought. Someone next to you in salah might raise their hands in a certain way while you don’t. These differences are natural and acceptable. But when ikhtilaf transforms into tafarruq - when disagreement morphs into division - that’s when the disease enters.

Tragically, we’ve become the exact opposite of this command. We take the smallest disagreements and use them as justification for our divisions. We completely fracture ourselves over minor differences of opinion. Allah ﷻ commands: don’t let anything cause division in the religion.

Then comes a difficult reality: كَبُرَ عَلَى ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ - “Difficult for the mushrikeen is that to which you invite them.” It’s very hard, a very big deal, for the idol worshipers to accept what you’re calling them to.

Imagine how this must have felt for the Prophet ﷺ. He’s doing his utmost to deliver the message of Islam, and people around him might be saying, “You’re trying to call them to this religion, but they’ll never change. It’s impossible. They’ll never listen.” But this isn’t just someone saying it - Allah ﷻ Himself is acknowledging: it’s too difficult for them to accept.

If this commentary comes from Allah ﷻ - that it’s too hard for the mushrikeen - why continue? What’s the point of this mission? The ayah anticipates this despair and immediately provides the answer: ٱللَّهُ يَجْتَبِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَآءُ - “Allah ﷻ chooses toward Himself whomever He wills.”

Your job is to convey the message. Bringing people to guidance is not your responsibility - that’s Allah’s ﷻ work. He does the choosing. Many of us found accepting Islam incredibly difficult, yet Allah ﷻ guided us. وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ - “And He guides to Himself whoever turns back to Him.” The door remains open for anyone who genuinely turns toward Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 14

وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ

And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord until a specified term, it would have been concluded between them. And indeed, those who were granted inheritance of the Scripture after them are in disquieting doubt about it.

The Prophet ﷺ has just been told that it’s too difficult for the mushrikeen to accept Islam. One might think: well, the mushrikeen are idol worshipers, but surely it will be easier with the Jews and Christians. They are nations of knowledge, people of scripture. They should recognize the truth when they see it.

Allah ﷻ addresses this assumption directly: وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ - “They did not fall into division until after knowledge came to them.”

This is a striking observation. The very knowledge that should have united them became the tool they used to divide themselves. The Jews divided into sects, the Christians divided into numerous factions, and Jews and Christians divided from each other. All of this happened after knowledge came to them, not before.

Why did this happen? بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ - “out of jealous animosity between themselves.” The word بَغْي means transgression driven by arrogance, pride and the urge to dominate. It’s the desire to break the rules just so you can win, just so you can get ahead. They wanted religious dominance over one another.

Ayah 14–27 · linked video: 02. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 14-27)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 14

Ayah 14

وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ

Allah ﷻ describes a group among the Jews and Christians who possess knowledge, but instead of using it to unite people upon truth, they weaponize it. They use their religious understanding to demonstrate superiority over other groups, to assert their legitimacy, to claim they alone own the authentic version of the faith. Religion becomes like market share in their hands—something to monopolize and control.

This phenomenon is not exclusive to past nations. The Muslim Ummah faces this same challenge. Some people of knowledge use religion to stake their claim, declaring, “We have the authentic religion. These other groups are deviant. They’re all going to the Fire. If you want to save yourself, listen to us. Don’t listen to anyone else. You better make sure you’re on the right path—and we own it personally.”

But Allah ﷻ exposes their true motivation. They’re not concerned with guiding people to the straight path. Their real interest lies in furthering their pride and ego. They want to position themselves at the top, above everyone else. They use religion to put other people down—بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ (out of transgression among themselves).

The ayah continues: وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ—“Had it not been for a word that preceded from your Master until a specified term, the matter would have been decided between them.” In other words, they would have been judged immediately. They would have been executed for the crime they committed: using Allah’s religion to further their pride and to fight against one another.

The most devastating consequence follows: وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ—“And indeed, those who were given inheritance of the Scripture after them are in disquieting doubt about it.” When the next generation of believers witnesses their elders fighting—this group against that group, this faction against that faction, this school of thought against that one, arguing about prayer styles, leadership based on ethnicity, creating divisions out of nothing—they lose faith in the religion itself.

When young Muslims grow up watching religious people constantly argue, they begin to think, “All these religious people do is fight. I don’t even know what’s so good about this religion, because everyone I know who practices it is full of pride and argumentation.” The infighting doesn’t strengthen faith; it breeds doubt that spreads from one generation to the next.

Ayah 15

فَلِذَٰلِكَ فَٱدْعُ ۖ وَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَقُلْ ءَامَنتُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ ۖ لَا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

After listing these overwhelming problems, Allah ﷻ addresses His Messenger ﷺ with remarkable instruction. Notice He doesn’t say فَلِتَلِكَ (and so, for that reason). He says فَلِذَٰلِكَ—“For these very reasons, then you should invite.” Anyone else hearing this litany of obstacles would respond, “These are the reasons I should not be doing this job.” But Allah ﷻ says the opposite: because these major problems exist, you are the man for the job.

The knowledgeable are arrogant. The Message is too hard for them to accept. The next generation is falling into doubt. These aren’t reasons for the Messenger ﷺ to step back—they’re precisely why he must step forward. That’s why you are who you are, Muhammad ﷺ. You are the one for this mission.

وَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ—“And stand firm just as you have been commanded.” We learned about standing firm in the previous surah, but now we discover a profound difference. When the Prophet ﷺ is commanded to stand firm, he must persevere in delivering this message no matter what happens, no matter how impossible the odds. Even when Allah ﷻ Himself describes how insurmountable the challenges are, the command remains: stand firm as you have been commanded.

وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ—“Don’t follow their empty, vain desires.”

وَقُلْ ءَامَنتُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ—“And declare: I have believed in whatever Allah has revealed of Scripture.” In the previous surah, the command was قُلْ إِنِّى مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (Say: Indeed I am of the Muslims). Now the declaration expands to affirm belief in all that Allah ﷻ has sent down.

وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ—“And I have been commanded to do justice among all of you”—among all these groups: Christians, Jews, Muslims, and those generations drowning in doubt. The first time Allah ﷻ mentioned judgment in this context, He said whatever you disagree about, take it back to Allah’s governance. But governance must be just, so the Messenger ﷺ becomes the judge.

The word عَدْل in Arabic refers to public, open, undisputed justice. It’s justice that everyone can see and verify. This establishes a core principle: the Messenger ﷺ came to establish justice among people.

Yet the Prophet ﷺ acknowledges the limits of worldly judgment: ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمُ—“Allah is our Master and yours.” As much as I strive to do justice among you, my justice cannot be absolute in this world. The Prophet ﷺ himself taught us this when he said that some people can make a better case than others, and he might rule based on what he hears. But the real judgment will come from Allah ﷻ.

لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ—“For us are our deeds, and for you are your deeds.” Our deeds will benefit us, and your deeds will benefit you. At the end of the day, the real evaluation comes from Allah ﷻ.

This becomes the interaction policy for Muslim efforts as well. One da’wah organization shouldn’t compete against another. One masjid shouldn’t see another masjid as a rival. One educational program shouldn’t view another as competition. When disagreements exist about secondary matters, they should be able to say to each other, “We’re doing what we’re doing, and you’re doing what you’re doing.”

لَا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ—“There is no argument between us and you.” We’re not here to make a case against you or to debate to prove who’s wrong and who’s right. That’s not the point.

ٱللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا—“Allah will bring us together.” We make du’a that we see eye to eye one day. Why not?

وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—“And to Him is the final return.” Whether we see eye to eye in this world or not, we all return to the same place.

This policy isn’t just for Muslims dealing with Christians and Jews—it’s how we should treat each other within the Ummah. One group to another, one masjid to another: “You’re doing great work for your community, and we’re not in competition. We’re not here to prove you started ’Eid on the wrong day or whatever else. Just leave it alone. There’s no point in arguing about these things.”

Ayah 16

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ٱسْتُجِيبَ لَهُۥ حُجَّتُهُمْ دَاحِضَةٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ

وَٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ٱسْتُجِيبَ لَهُۥ—“And those who keep debating about Allah and keep making arguments about Him even after the call has been responded to.” They continue arguing even after the truth has been made clear.

حُجَّتُهُمْ دَاحِضَةٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ—“Their arguments are refuted with their Master.” The word دَاحِضَة means waste, something with no benefit or value. All their efforts toward undermining the truth are worthless. All the arguments they construct have no good coming from them—they’re just waste as far as their Master is concerned.

وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ—“And upon them is rage”—anger heaped exclusively upon them.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ—“And they will have intense punishment.”

Ayah 17

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۗ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ—“Allah is the One who sent down the Book with truth.” The Book came with purpose, with justice built into it.

وَٱلْمِيزَانَ—“And the Scale.” This is the third concept of justice in these ayat. First there was حُكْم (judgment), then عَدْل (justice), and now مِيزَان (the scale). Notice how many times Allah ﷻ emphasizes that this religion came down to establish justice between people.

وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ—“And what would make you perceive? Perhaps the Hour is near.” The word قَرِيب (near) is used here, bringing the reality of the Last Day into sharp focus.

Ayah 18

يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهَا ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْهَا وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُمَارُونَ فِى ٱلسَّاعَةِ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهَا—“Those who don’t believe in it are trying to rush the Hour.” They mock it: “Okay, you keep talking about the Hour. Let’s see it. Where is it?”

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْهَا—“And those who believe are terrified of it.” The word مُشْفِقُونَ conveys deep fear and apprehension. Believers don’t rush toward that Day—they prepare for it with reverence and caution.

وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا ٱلْحَقُّ—“And they know with certainty that it is the truth.” There’s no doubt in their hearts about its reality.

أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُمَارُونَ فِى ٱلسَّاعَةِ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ—“No doubt about it, those who argue based on doubt about the Hour are in misguidance that is far out there.” The word يُمَارُونَ ) comes from مِرَاء , which we saw at the end of the previous surah. It means to argue based on doubt—not based on conviction, but on uncertainty. “I’m not so sure the Hour is going to happen, so let me start an argument about it.” These people are described as being in ضَلَـٰل بَعِيد—a misguidance that is distant, way out there, far from the truth.

Ayah 19

ٱللَّهُ لَطِيفٌۢ بِعِبَادِهِۦ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ

ٱللَّهُ لَطِيفٌۢ بِعِبَادِهِۦ—“Allah is subtle and caring toward His slaves.” Even the people who talk trash about the Hour, about Allah ﷻ, about this religion—

يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ—“He provides for whoever He wants.” He still gives them provision. He still allows them to live.

وَهُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ—“And He is the Ultimately Mighty, the Final Authority.” Allah ﷻ has the might and authority to annihilate them right now, but He’s لَطِيف—subtle and caring with His slaves. He takes it easy. He gives them time. He provides for them even as they reject Him.

Ayah 20

مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُۥ فِى حَرْثِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِن نَّصِيبٍ

Allah ﷻ now describes our efforts in this world using the metaphor of farming. Whatever you and I do for Jannah is like a farmer planting seed, tilling the soil, working at it continuously. But harvest season only comes in the afterlife.

مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُۥ فِى حَرْثِهِۦ—“Whoever desires the harvest of the Hereafter, We will increase for him his harvest.” He plants something small, but Allah ﷻ multiplies it. One good deed becomes ten, becomes seven hundred, becomes countless rewards.

وَمَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا—“And whoever desires the harvest of this world, We will give him something from it.” Notice it doesn’t say “We’ll give him all of it”—just مِنْهَا, some portion of it. You put in all this effort—maybe you work to start a business, but the growth isn’t 100%. It’s 20%, maybe 30%. You never reach your full material potential. You get a portion of your efforts returned to you in this world.

وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِن نَّصِيبٍ—“And he will have no portion whatsoever in the Hereafter.” This person who worked only for material gain will have absolutely nothing waiting for him in the next life. No share. No portion. Nothing.

Ayah 21

أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ شَرَعُوا۟ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا لَمْ يَأْذَنۢ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةُ ٱلْفَصْلِ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ شَرَعُوا۟ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا لَمْ يَأْذَنۢ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ—“Or do they have partners who have legislated for them from the religion what Allah has not permitted at all?” They’ve created their own religious laws, their own system, without any authorization from Allah ﷻ.

وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةُ ٱلْفَصْلِ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ—“And had it not been for the decisive word, the matter would have been judged between them already.” This is another word for justice—فَصْل , decisive judgment. The word that was passed long ago delays their immediate punishment.

وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ—“And indeed, the wrongdoers will have exclusively for themselves painful punishment.”

Ayah 22

تَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ وَهُوَ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى رَوْضَاتِ ٱلْجَنَّاتِ ۖ لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ

تَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟—“You will see the wrongdoers terrified because of what they’ve earned.” Earlier, Allah ﷻ mentioned that believers are مُشْفِقُونَ (terrified) of the Last Day, while disbelievers want to rush to it mockingly. But a time is coming when those wrongdoers will be the terrified ones.

وَهُوَ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ—“And it will fall upon them.” The punishment they denied will come crashing down on them.

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى رَوْضَاتِ ٱلْجَنَّاتِ—“And those who believed and did righteous deeds will be in the gardens of Paradise.” The word رَوْضَات refers to beautiful gardens with water fountains and flowers so vibrant their colors overwhelm the senses. These are the most spectacular parts of Jannah.

لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ—“They will have whatever they want from their Master.” Whatever thought enters their mind, they’ll have it.

ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ—“That is the ultimate favor.” That is the great bounty from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 23

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يُبَشِّرُ ٱللَّهُ عِبَادَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا ٱلْمَوَدَّةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ ۗ وَمَن يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَّزِدْ لَهُۥ فِيهَا حُسْنًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يُبَشِّرُ ٱللَّهُ عِبَادَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ—“That is what Allah gives as good news to His slaves—those who believe and do righteous deeds.”

قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا ٱلْمَوَدَّةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ—“Say: I don’t ask you for any compensation for it except affection based on kinship.” The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to tell his own tribe: “I’m not asking you for money or reward. But you are my people. At least show me the courtesy and love that is owed to me as a member of your own tribe. That much, at least, you should show me.”

The implication is devastating—they don’t even show him that basic respect. They can’t even uphold their own tribal principles when it comes to him. He’s asking for the bare minimum: “If I’m your family member, at least give me a respectful listen. Sit down without joking around and mocking. Give me that much respect. You can’t even give me that?”

The word مَوَدَّة is significant here. As opposed to وُدّ , which is simple love, مَوَدَّة suggests extreme love, the kind of devotion Arab tribes had for their own members. They would sacrifice themselves for their tribespeople. The Prophet ﷺ is saying, “I’m not asking for anything different than what you give every other tribe member. You have mawaddah for everyone else. Why don’t you give me some? Where is the love when it comes to me?”

وَمَن يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَّزِدْ لَهُۥ فِيهَا حُسْنًا—“And whoever earns a good deed, We will increase for him goodness in it.” The word يَقْتَرِفْ is fascinating. It literally means to peel the scab off a wound. In Arabic expression, اِقْتِرَاف is typically used for earning something evil, something disgusting, something criminal. When you commit a crime or do something shameful, that’s called اِقْتِرَاف.

But here Allah ﷻ uses this word for حَسَنَة (a good deed). It’s as if to say: whoever earns even the tiniest good deed—something so small it barely qualifies as good, almost more like a bad deed but just barely crossing into good—We will increase for him goodness in it. A minuscule good deed, and Allah ﷻ multiplies it beyond imagination.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ—“Indeed, Allah is Extremely Forgiving, Extremely Grateful.” Grateful here means appreciative. Allah ﷻ appreciates even your smallest efforts and rewards them abundantly.

Ayah 24

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۖ فَإِن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ ۗ وَيَمْحُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا—“Or do they say he has invented a lie against Allah?”

فَإِن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ—“If Allah had wanted, He would have placed a seal upon your heart.” This echoes what Allah ﷻ says about hypocrites and those who fabricate lies against Him. Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: if you had done what they’re accusing you of, He would have sealed your heart just as He seals theirs.

وَيَمْحُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ—“And Allah erases falsehood.” He wipes it out, annihilates it completely.

وَيُحِقُّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦٓ—“And He establishes the truth with His words.” The word يُحِقُّ means to make something known as truth, to prove its truthfulness beyond doubt.

إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ—“Indeed, He is fully knowledgeable of what is in the chests.” These accusations they’re making up—Allah ﷻ knows how much of this is just them trying to construct a lame excuse, and how much they might actually believe. He knows the true nature of what lies in their hearts.

Ayah 25

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ—“And He is the One who accepts repentance from His slaves.” No matter what they’ve done, the door of repentance remains open.

وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ—“And He pardons many evil deeds.” So many ugly deeds, so many crimes—He overlooks them when someone turns back to Him.

وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ—“And He knows what you do.” Even the things you do mindlessly, not even realizing what you’re up to—He knows.

Ayah 26

وَيَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ

وَيَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ—“And He responds to those who believe and do good deeds.” Allah ﷻ answers the call of believers who couple their faith with righteous action.

وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ—“And He gives them even more from His favor.” They ask for something, and Allah ﷻ gives them what they asked for plus more out of His generosity.

وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ—“And the disbelievers—for them is severe punishment.”

Ayah 27

وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

This is a remarkable ayah that reveals a profound social reality. Allah ﷻ tells us what would happen in society if certain conditions were different.

وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ—“And if Allah had expanded provision for His slaves”—if Allah ﷻ had made everyone rich, if everyone was a millionaire, if everyone had everything they wanted—

لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—“they would have rebelled in the land.” They would have committed all kinds of crimes and corruption.

Think about this: we only have a very small minority of extremely wealthy people in the world, yet a huge chunk of the world’s corruption and crime comes from them. The ultra-wealthy have disproportionate influence over the suffering and injustice that exists globally.

You know how in Islam we have the concept of حَسَنَات جَارِيَة —continuous good deeds? A father teaches Islam to his child, that child teaches their child, and so on. The reward traces back through the generations to the original person who started that chain of good. Crime works the same way. You see crime in the streets, but often you can trace it back to policies set by the very wealthy—policies that facilitate those crimes.

Yes, the individual criminal is responsible for his own actions. But you cannot completely absolve the policymakers, the big businesses that drained all the small businesses from a neighborhood, the real estate developers, or the tax policies that prevented schools from having quality education. Crime trickles down, and most of the time it trickles from the very top to the very bottom.

Consider a straightforward example: the alcohol industry. Companies like Budweiser and Coors are multibillion-dollar brewing industries. How much crime in the streets results directly from alcohol? When a liquor store opens in a neighborhood, other crimes spike immediately. Some counties in Texas were dry counties—you could go to Walmart and not see Budweiser, you could go to 7-Eleven and it wouldn’t be there. Then ordinances were passed and those counties stopped being dry. Just examine crime statistics before and after—there’s a world of difference.

When these restrictions are lifted, wealthy corporations benefit at their bottom line, but they don’t care about the crime or suffering that follows. Certain ordinances get passed, people suffer, but the profits flow upward.

This is what Allah ﷻ means: if everyone had unlimited wealth, the rebellion and corruption would be unimaginable.

وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ—“But He sends down according to a measure as He wills.” Allah ﷻ distributes provision in precise measures, according to His wisdom.

إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ—“Indeed, He is, concerning His slaves, All-Aware, All-Seeing.” He knows exactly what each person needs, what each society can handle, and He sees the consequences of every distribution of wealth. His wisdom in how He apportions provision is perfect, even when we cannot perceive the reasons behind it.

Ayah 27–36 · linked video: 03. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 27-36)

Surah Ash-Shura: Ayahs 27-36

Ayah 27

۞ وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

Allah ﷻ reveals a profound wisdom about provision and human nature in this ayah. If He were to expand provision for all His servants, if everyone were wealthy and had unlimited resources, they would transgress in the earth. The word بَغَوْا refers to rebellion and transgression. When people are absorbed in wealth, many become heedless of their purpose and responsibilities.

Consider the reality of work and daily obligations. People spend eight or ten hours working, commute two hours back home, then have limited time for rest and recreation. This structure actually serves as a mercy from Allah ﷻ. If all their time were free, if they had no need to earn a living, the world would witness corruption like never before. The requirement to work, to earn provisions, to meet obligations keeps society functioning and restrains much potential harm.

This is why we see that weekends—when people are off work—often witness the highest rates of car accidents and crime. Emergency rooms fill up Friday and Saturday nights. Imagine if every day were like a weekend, if people had unlimited free time combined with unlimited wealth. The earth would be filled with chaos and rebellion.

Instead, Allah ﷻ sends down provisions بِقَدَرٍ , in measured amounts, according to His wisdom. He distributes whatever He wills in precisely the quantities that serve His greater plan. The ayah concludes by affirming that He is خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ regarding His slaves—He has complete knowledge and He sees everything fully. His limitation of provision is not from stinginess but from perfect knowledge of what is best for His creation.

Ayah 28

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ وَيَنشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُۥ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ

He is the One who sends down الْغَيْثَ , the rain, in just the right amount. The timing described here is particularly significant: مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ , after people had lost all hope. When the drought has extended, when pessimism has set in, when people think relief will never come—that is precisely when Allah’s ﷻ mercy arrives.

He distributes His mercy, spreading it across His creation. The rain becomes a physical manifestation of divine mercy reaching the parched earth. This ayah identifies Allah ﷻ with two beautiful names: الْوَلِىُّ , the Protective Guardian who takes care of His creation’s affairs, and الْحَمِيدُ , the One who is inherently praiseworthy, needing no praise because He is praised in and of Himself.

Ayah 29

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَثَّ فِيهِمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذَا يَشَآءُ قَدِيرٌ

Among His miraculous signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and whatever He has spread throughout both of them من دَآبَّةٍ , of every kind of creature. The word دَآبَّةٍ encompasses all moving creatures—the birds in the air, the animals on the earth, everything that moves and lives.

The ayah culminates with a reminder of resurrection: وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذَا يَشَآءُ قَدِيرٌ—He is completely capable of gathering them all together whenever He wills. The same Power that spread these countless creatures across the vastness of creation can gather them all again. This is a clear reference to the Day of Judgment when all creation will be assembled.

Ayah 30

وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ

This ayah addresses a question that troubles many hearts: Why do calamities happen to us? Allah ﷻ provides the answer with remarkable clarity and mercy. Whatever مُّصِيبَةٍ , whatever calamity or difficulty strikes you, it is because of what your own hands have earned. Our trials are consequences of our own actions.

But the ayah doesn’t end there. It continues with profound mercy: وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ —and He overlooks much. This is critical to understand. When someone complains, “Why is this happening to me?”—the reality is that what they’re experiencing is only a fraction of what they’ve actually earned. Allah ﷻ is overlooking far more than what He’s allowing them to experience.

If Allah ﷻ gave the full consequence of our actions, we would stop complaining about our current difficulties and start begging for them back. We would be thanking Him for His mercy in limiting our trials. Think of a parent who denies a child candy, and the child complains endlessly. The parent might respond, “You’re fortunate. I was considering a real punishment. This is the least I’m doing.” That’s the relationship here—what we face is minimal compared to what we deserve, and Allah ﷻ overlooks so much more.

Ayah 31

وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ

You are not going to overpower or escape anyone in the land. The word مُعْجِزِينَ carries the meaning of rendering someone powerless or escaping their grasp. No human being can escape Allah’s ﷻ decree or overpower His will.

Furthermore, you will not find any protective guardian (وَلِىٍّ) nor any helper (نَصِيرٍ,) besides Allah ﷻ. When His decision comes, when His decree unfolds, there is no power in creation that can shield you or assist you against it. This is a call to complete reliance on Allah ﷻ alone.

Ayah 32

وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلْجَوَارِ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ

Among His miraculous signs are الْجَوَارِ , the flowing, sailing ships in the middle of the ocean. These vessels appear كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ , like towering mountains or flags. The image is striking—massive ships cutting through the vast ocean, standing tall like landmarks visible from great distances.

This was particularly miraculous in the time of revelation, when sailing technology was far less advanced than today. Yet even with modern ships, the sight remains awe-inspiring. These floating mountains of steel and cargo traverse the oceans, a testament to Allah’s ﷻ signs.

Ayah 33

إِن يَشَأْ يُسْكِنِ ٱلرِّيحَ فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَوَاكِدَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ

If He willed, He could still the winds completely. The result? فَيَظْلَلْنَ رَوَاكِدَ —the ships would remain motionless, stranded on the back of the ocean. Those mighty vessels that seemed so powerful would become helpless, unable to move forward or return home, simply floating in place.

The ayah concludes by identifying who truly benefits from these signs: لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ —for everyone who is extremely patient and extremely grateful. These are the people who see these signs and understand them properly. They recognize their complete dependence on Allah ﷻ and respond with patience in difficulty and gratitude in ease.

Ayah 34

أَوْ يُوبِقْهُنَّ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَيَعْفُ عَن كَثِيرٍ

Or He could دrown them, turn the ships over entirely. The word يُوبِقْهُنَّ means to destroy or drown them. And why would this happen? بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ —because of what they earned with their own actions.

Yet again, the ayah ends with mercy: وَيَعْفُ عَن كَثِيرٍ —He forgives and overlooks much. Even those on ships in the middle of the ocean, whose lives hang by a thread, are only experiencing consequences for a small portion of what they’ve done. Allah ﷻ overlooks so much more.

Ayah 35

وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُجَـٰدِلُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ

This ayah addresses those who debate and argue against Allah’s ﷻ signs. When that ship is about to sink, when the winds have stopped or the storm has come, where can they hide? What argument will save them then? The word مَّحِيصٍ means a place of escape or refuge.

Let them learn through these experiences that they have no escape. When Allah’s ﷻ decree comes, all their arguments become meaningless. This is preparation for understanding that on the Day of Judgment, there will be no escape whatsoever.

Ayah 36

فَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ

Whatever you have been given is merely متاع , temporary utilities and enjoyment of this worldly life. This ayah requires us to examine everything we possess and value. Allah ﷻ wants us to compare it with what He has prepared, and He gives us two qualities to consider: خَيْرٌ —it is better, and أَبْقَىٰ —it is longer lasting.

You have a car? Allah ﷻ has a better vehicle for you in Jannah, and it’s longer lasting. You have a house here? Allah’s ﷻ house for you is better and longer lasting. Everything you enjoy in this life should be viewed through this lens. First, acknowledge that this is not the best there is. Second, recognize that it’s not going to last forever.

This is a difficult reality to internalize. When you walk into your home, can you truly think, “This home is not forever”? When you’re with your children, your spouse, your parents, can you genuinely think, “This time together is temporary”? It’s difficult to live with that awareness, but it’s the truth we must embrace. We must tell ourselves with full conviction: what Allah ﷻ has is better and longer lasting.

This perspective is لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ —for those who believed and for those who completely relied on their Master. Those who don’t rely on Allah ﷻ think differently. They prefer this world. They believe this is all there is, so they live entirely for what they can get right now. They don’t think about anything better or longer lasting afterwards.

But the believers know better. They work for this life while keeping their eyes on what truly matters—the eternal reward that is both superior in quality and infinite in duration.

Ayah 36–38 · linked video: 04. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 36-38)

Surah Ash-Shura: Ayahs 36-38

Ayah 36

فَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَمَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَمَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ

“Whatever you have been granted is merely the temporary enjoyment of this worldly life. But what Allah has is better and more lasting for those who believe and place their trust in their Lord.”

This passage represents one of the most profound statements in the Quran about attitude and worldview. Allah ﷻ tells us that whatever we possess in this world—wealth, status, comfort—is simply متاع الحياة الدنيا , utilities for worldly life. The word متاع implies something temporary, something we use but don’t ultimately keep.

The beauty of this ayah lies in its contrast: “Whatever Allah ﷻ has is better and longer lasting.” This isn’t just information—it’s meant to shape how we think. The believer who internalizes this develops a firm conviction that becomes inseparable from their personality. They never forget about the akhirah, even while living fully in this world.

It’s important to understand what this doesn’t mean. Some people assume that thinking about the akhirah means abandoning concern for dunya altogether. This is a misunderstanding. The Prophet ﷺ thought about dunya matters—he just never forgot about the akhirah. It becomes a subconscious part of your worldview, a constant presence in how you evaluate choices and opportunities.

The real test comes in moments of temptation. Sometimes dunya presents itself in ways that are extremely attractive—a lucrative but impermissible business deal, an opportunity that requires compromising your values, a shortcut that violates Islamic principles. At that precise moment, your belief in this ayah will be tested. Do you truly believe that what Allah ﷻ has is better, even though you haven’t seen it yet? This is where trust becomes action.

Consider the young person challenged daily by inappropriate content online. Or the family facing financial hardship who receives approval for a riba-based loan that promises to solve all their problems immediately. The temptation to sign those papers can be overwhelming—all your difficulties seemingly disappearing with one signature. But at that moment, holding firm to “what Allah ﷻ has is better” requires deep conviction.

The same test appears in decisions about education. Your child gets accepted into a prestigious school, but you’d need to take on massive student loans with compounding interest. The pressure is immense—it’s a great school with a recognized name. But if you step back and consider the reality, that young person might graduate with $150,000 in debt, start a career making perhaps $50,000 a year, and find that $35,000 of that barely covers basic living expenses. The remaining $15,000 won’t even make a dent in the loan principal while interest continues accumulating. They’ve started their adult life buried in debt. Yet at the moment of decision, it seems like the only reasonable choice.

This principle extends to family decisions as well. You want to hold a simple wedding for your daughter—conduct the nikah at the masjid, arrange modest catering, keep everything within your means. But the pressure builds: “Why aren’t you getting a DJ? Didn’t you see your cousin’s wedding? What will people say? What will your uncle think?” If you cave to that pressure and organize a lavish celebration, you burden the newly married couple with debt that follows them for years—all for an event that people will forget within three days. The debt, however, they’ll remember.

The phrase وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ —“and upon their Lord they place their trust”—captures this essential quality. True tawakkul means trusting what Allah ﷻ tells us is better, even when we cannot yet see it. The Quran calls toward the beauty of simplicity, of living within our means, of not placing ourselves and our families in unnecessary hardship.

Ayah 37

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ وَإِذَا مَا غَضِبُوا۟ هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ

“And those who avoid the major sins and shameful deeds, and when they become angry, they forgive.”

This ayah establishes crucial priorities for believers. Allah ﷻ doesn’t describe people obsessing over every minor detail of religious practice, becoming so perfectionistic that they develop spiritual anxiety. Instead, He identifies what truly matters: avoiding the كَبَائِر —the major sins.

The word يَجْتَنِبُونَ means to sidestep, to get away from, to avoid completely. These believers distance themselves from riba, from earning through haram means, from consuming haram, from looking at what’s forbidden, from stealing and murder—the serious violations that corrupt the soul and harm society.

But notice something significant: after mentioning major sins, Allah ﷻ adds وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ —shameful deeds. This word appears in plural form, indicating multiple types and levels of shamelessness. If Allah ﷻ had only said “avoid major sins,” classical scholars would have naturally understood that zina falls under that category. But by specifically adding fawahish, Allah ﷻ emphasizes: you must be especially vigilant about this.

Why the extra emphasis on shamelessness? Because it operates in layers. It’s not always direct and obvious. Someone might say, “I’m just going to a party—parties aren’t haram.” But one thing leads to another. First it’s just music. Then it’s certain types of music with explicit lyrics. Those words create images in the mind. Those images prompt you to search for the video. Then you start browsing for more. What started as “just music” becomes a gateway to visual content your eyes shouldn’t see. And if this song is playing live somewhere, maybe you attend that concert, then a club. This is how Shaytan works—يَنزِعُ —he draws the bucket out of the well slowly, gradually, little by little.

Remember how Shaytan approached our parents (AS) in Jannah. He didn’t present them with one direct temptation they immediately accepted. No, he worked gradually, incrementally, persistently. That’s the nature of fawahish—it comes in layers and degrees, and we must guard against all of them.

Then comes a remarkable quality: وَإِذَا مَا غَضِبُوا۟ هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ —“when they become angry, they immediately forgive.” This reveals the progression of spiritual development embedded in this ayah.

These three qualities follow a deliberate sequence, teaching us about proper proportion and priorities. Think about any job—you have multiple responsibilities, various projects with different deadlines and levels of importance. A good employee understands which tasks demand immediate attention and which can wait. They don’t treat everything as equally urgent; they recognize priorities.

The same principle applies in religion. If you want to be a strong believer, someone who truly trusts Allah ﷻ, here’s your roadmap: First, stay away from major sins. Before discussing voluntary fasts on Mondays and Thursdays, before planning to pray extra night prayers, before making plans for Hajj or Umrah—all of which are beautiful acts—take care of the major requirements first. Distance yourself from major sins.

Second, protect yourself from shamelessness. Guard your character vigilantly. This is critical because shamelessness attacks the heart directly. The more you expose yourself to it, the more your heart becomes spiritually hardened. You’ll find yourself unable to cry in prayer, unable to feel genuine emotion during Quran recitation. Your heart loses its softness because fawahish eats away at spiritual sensitivity.

But when you successfully guard yourself from shamelessness, your heart begins to soften. And as your heart softens, you reach a profound level of spiritual development: even in moments when your heart should be hard—when you’re angry—it remains soft enough to forgive.

In Arabic, when someone is angry, they’re called قاسي القلب —hard-hearted. Allah ﷻ is saying that if you protect yourself from shamelessness, your heart will become so soft that even in anger, when hardness is expected, you think: “What Allah ﷻ has for me is better than my anger. I’m going to forgive. I’m going to let it go.”

This represents an incredible internal transformation. You don’t need elaborate spiritual exercises or secret techniques to feel closer to Allah ﷻ. People often ask, “How can I feel more connection in prayer? Why don’t I cry like others do? Why don’t I feel anything?” The answer may lie in these fundamentals: Are major sins still present? Is shamelessness still there? Has anger not been brought under control? Learn to forgive, and your heart will automatically soften.

Ayah 38

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورَىٰ بَيْنَهُمْ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ

“And those who respond to their Lord, establish prayer, conduct their affairs by mutual consultation, and spend from what We have provided them.”

After establishing individual spiritual qualities, Allah ﷻ now transitions to communal responsibility. Notice the progression: we moved from personal accountability—controlling major sins, avoiding shamelessness, managing anger—to collective action. This shift is deliberate and profound.

وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ )—“they establish prayer.” This phrase doesn’t refer to praying alone. إِقَامَةُ الصَّلَاةِ specifically implies congregational prayer. We pray together, united behind one leader.

The wisdom here is extraordinary. Consider what happens in congregational prayer: the imam leads, and you follow. Let’s say it’s Maghrib—you’re supposed to pray three rak’ahs. But the imam mistakenly stands for a fourth. What do you do? You say “SubhanAllah” to alert him to the error. That’s your responsibility—to let him know. But here’s what’s remarkable: even after alerting him to his mistake, what must you do? You still follow him. You continue behind him.

At first glance, this might seem like blind obedience, even dictatorship. The religion says pray three, the imam prayed four, yet you’re instructed to follow. But look deeper at what’s happening. You made the correction—you said SubhanAllah. At that point, you’ve fulfilled your duty. You’re no longer answerable to Allah ﷻ for that error. If asked, “Why did you pray four?” you can say, “I said SubhanAllah. I did my part.” You’re absolved. The responsibility now rests with the imam; he’s the one answerable.

This mechanism within salah itself teaches the principles of شُورَىٰ (shura). The community has the right—indeed, the duty—to correct their leader when he exceeds proper limits or crosses boundaries. When the leader is engaged in obeying Allah ﷻ and makes an error, the people must speak up. They must let him know. This is their role.

But equally important: they cannot break away. They cannot simply walk away and start their own separate congregation, saying, “This imam made a mistake in the fourth rak’ah, so I’m starting a different jama’ah now.” No, you must stay unified.

Similarly, if the imam makes a mistake in Quran recitation, what should you do? Correct him. And if he doesn’t accept the correction? Correct him again. Then when he goes into ruku’, you follow him into ruku’. You don’t pull him back and take his place. You cannot do that.

These are the embedded principles of mutual consultation. The first prerequisite? You must have your anger under control. You must have distanced yourself from major sins. This is crucial because it represents an amazing transition from individual responsibility to communal responsibility. Before you can participate effectively in collective decision-making, you need to know where you stand personally. Most importantly: where’s your temper?

Anyone who has participated in shura board meetings understands this wisdom intimately. If you haven’t, you might not realize how much anger surfaces in these settings, how much rage can emerge during collective decision-making. This is precisely why Allah ﷻ established the prerequisite: وَإِذَا مَا غَضِبُوا۟ هُمْ يَغْفِرُونَ )—master forgiveness in anger first. Only then are you ready for وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورَىٰ بَيْنَهُمْ —“their matters are conducted by mutual consultation.”

The phrase أَمْرُهُمْ —“their matters”—deserves reflection. What matters? Allah ﷻ has given this Ummah a mission, a responsibility to live according to this deen and share it with others. We must let the world know what this religion truly represents. But this vast responsibility breaks down into countless smaller projects: a youth program here, a masjid there, a conference, educational initiatives. Each represents a small part of the larger mission.

In every one of these projects, Muslims must learn to work together effectively. This means learning to work with each other’s mistakes, with different personality styles, with varying perspectives and approaches. You cannot accomplish this without open, honest communication between people working toward the same goal.

This represents a critical aspect of religious guidance that deserves independent study. Just as Allah ﷻ provides guidance for individual spiritual development, He provides communal guidance—direction for how Muslims should come together and cooperate. How do we make Islamic organizational work function better? What are the ethics of collective action? How should Muslims navigate disagreements while maintaining unity?

The principles are embedded right here in these ayahs: discipline, unified leadership, the right and responsibility to offer correction, the obligation to maintain unity, and above all, the spiritual prerequisites that make healthy collaboration possible. Establish prayer together, conduct your affairs through mutual consultation, and from what Allah ﷻ has provided, spend in His way. These aren’t separate commands—they’re interconnected principles that create communities capable of fulfilling the mission Allah ﷻ has entrusted to this Ummah.

Ayah 38–44 · linked video: 05. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 38-44)

Surah Ash-Shura: Ayahs 38-44

Ayah 38

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَمْرُهُمْ شُورَىٰ بَيْنَهُمْ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ

This ayah establishes the foundational characteristics of a believing community. Allah ﷻ describes those who respond to their Lord, establish prayer, conduct their affairs through mutual consultation, and spend from what they have been provided.

The concept of شُورَىٰ , mutual consultation, sits at the heart of how Muslims should function collectively. This principle ensures that decisions are never made unilaterally. There must always be discussion before decision, always open channels of communication where opinions are genuinely heard and considered.

True shura means you don’t simply invite people to speak so they can voice their concerns and feel heard, only to disregard their input entirely. It’s not about humoring people by letting them “blow off steam” while you’ve already made your decision. Genuine consultation means you listen with the intent to understand and incorporate wisdom from others.

The importance of this principle is underscored by the fact that even the Prophet ﷺ, who received direct revelation from Allah ﷻ, was commanded to consult with the believers. If anyone in human history had the authority to make decisions without consultation, it was the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Yet Allah ﷻ commanded him directly in Surah Al-Imran: وَشَاوِرْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ (and consult them in the matter). When the one person who truly doesn’t need consultation is commanded to engage in it, what does that tell us about everyone else?

This ayah teaches us that leadership in Islam requires humility. No matter your position, you need the perspective, wisdom and insights of others. The moment you think you’re above consultation is the moment you’ve disconnected from this essential Islamic principle.

After establishing shura, the ayah mentions spending: وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (and from what We have provided them, they spend). Notice Allah ﷻ didn’t simply say “from the wealth We gave them” but rather “from what We have provided them.” This encompasses far more than money. It includes time, energy, youth, skills, comfort and priorities. Different people have different capacities. For some, writing a check is easy, but sacrificing time is difficult. For others, money comes hard, but they’ll give their time freely. The believer recognizes that whatever Allah ﷻ has given them becomes a resource to invest in serving Him.

Ayah 39

وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْبَغْىُ هُمْ يَنتَصِرُونَ

This ayah introduces a crucial distinction in Islamic teaching about responding to aggression. After describing individual characteristics in the preceding ayahs, Allah ﷻ now addresses the collective level: those who, when struck by transgression and domineering force, defend themselves and fight back.

This represents a fundamental philosophical principle in our religion. At the individual level, we are taught to be forgiving. But at the collective level, at the level of community and governance, there exists a right to defend and strike back.

Understanding this distinction is essential. The Makkan period of the Prophet’s ﷺ life provides the perfect illustration. During those years, Muslims endured severe persecution. Bilal (RA) had his back burned. The family of Yasir (RA) was tortured unto death. Yet the command was consistent: hold back your hands, be patient. The Prophet ﷺ sanctioned no violence whatsoever in Makkah.

Why this restraint? Because in Makkah, Muslims existed as a group, not as a community with governance. The critical difference between Makkah and Madinah wasn’t just geographic. In Madinah, Muslims had law and order, a court system, governance structures, and the makings of a state. The sanction to engage in defensive violence came only when Muslims had these institutional frameworks in place.

This principle carries profound implications. At the individual level, if someone strikes you and runs away, you cannot chase them down and retaliate. That’s not self-defense—that’s revenge, and it makes you a criminal under any just legal system. You can only defend yourself in the moment of attack. Retaliation, by contrast, is never permitted at the individual level. It can only be sanctioned at the governmental level.

The wisdom in this is multilayered. If group-level retaliation were permitted, Muslims would never establish stable, civil societies. Every neighborhood, every community center, every small gathering would potentially become a flashpoint for violence. The very fact that Muslim civilizations endured for centuries as stable governmental societies demonstrates that we never understood violence as something sanctioned at the group level.

This prophetic model of non-violent protest during oppression has proven its effectiveness repeatedly throughout history. When we look at successful, sustainable movements for change in recent times, they share this characteristic. Non-violent mass movements bring about transformations that last because they don’t create cycles of revenge and counter-revenge.

Ayah 40

وَجَزَٰٓؤُا۟ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِّثْلُهَا ۖ فَمَنْ عَفَا وَأَصْلَحَ فَأَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ establishes a remarkable balance in this ayah. The compensation for an evil act is an equivalent response—justice demands proportionality. Yet immediately, the door to something higher opens: whoever pardons and reconciles receives a reward that can only be described as being “upon Allah.”

This phrasing is extraordinary. Usually when Allah ﷻ describes rewards, He specifies them: gardens, rivers, mansions in Paradise. But here, the compensation for forgiveness is so immense that it defies description. The only way to express it is to say فَأَجْرُهُۥ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ—his reward is upon Allah, meaning Allah ﷻ Himself will take care of it in ways beyond our imagination.

Think about someone you struggle to forgive. Perhaps a cousin who wronged you, a friend who betrayed your trust. Your mind immediately objects: “But they don’t deserve forgiveness.” That’s precisely the point. If they deserved it, it wouldn’t be forgiveness—it would just be acknowledging their innocence. Forgiveness is given to those who don’t deserve it, just as Allah ﷻ forgives us not because we deserve it, but out of His mercy.

The ayah concludes with an important clarification: إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (Indeed, He does not love the wrongdoers). This serves multiple purposes. First, it warns that taking revenge beyond what’s justified makes you a wrongdoer yourself. You were struck once, so striking back three times isn’t justice. Second, and equally important, it prevents a misunderstanding. Don’t think that because Allah ﷻ encourages you to forgive the criminal, He therefore loves the criminal. The wrongdoer remains a wrongdoer in Allah’s sight. Islam promotes forgiveness at the individual level while maintaining that wrong is still wrong.

Ayah 41

وَلَمَنِ ٱنتَصَرَ بَعْدَ ظُلْمِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مَا عَلَيْهِم مِّن سَبِيلٍ

This ayah provides essential clarity and protection for those who have been wronged. Whoever seeks justice after being wronged—those people have no case against them. There is no sin, no blame, no سَبِيل (path of reproach) upon them.

This is Islam’s balanced approach. While forgiveness brings immeasurable spiritual reward, pursuing justice is completely permissible. You are not sinful if you take someone to court for a legitimate grievance. You should not be pressured or shamed with statements like “You should forgive, brother.” No—you have every right to pursue justice through proper channels.

However, this doesn’t mean every situation requires the full weight of retaliation. You have two options before you. First, you can forgive, motivated by the knowledge that Allah ﷻ will compensate you with unimaginable reward. Second, you can pursue justice, and in doing so, you commit no sin. No one can make a case against you for choosing this path. Both doors are open to you, and Islam honors both choices while highlighting the spiritual excellence of the first.

Ayah 42

إِنَّمَا ٱلسَّبِيلُ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَظْلِمُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ وَيَبْغُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

The case, the blame, the path of punishment—this is reserved exclusively for those who wrong people and rebel in the land without right or justification. These are the ones who face painful punishment, both in this world’s justice system and in the Hereafter.

This ayah protects against a common manipulation. Sometimes Muslims wrong other Muslims—stealing from them, cheating them in business—and when the victim seeks justice, the perpetrator appeals to religious brotherhood. “We’re believers, we’re brothers, you should forgive me. Don’t take me to court.” They appeal to mutual friends: “Tell him not to sue me. Tell him to show mercy, especially since it’s Ramadan.”

But notice: the wrongdoer won’t return what they stole. They simply don’t want to face consequences. This is taking advantage of the religion, twisting its beautiful teachings about forgiveness into a shield for injustice. Allah ﷻ protects against this. You have every religious right to pursue justice. Don’t be emotionally manipulated into thinking there’s some religious obligation to let wrongdoing go unpunished.

At the same time, the door of forgiveness remains open, with its tremendous spiritual compensation. The choice is yours, and both choices are dignified by Islam.

Ayah 43

وَلَمَن صَبَرَ وَغَفَرَ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ

For the one who remains patient and forgives, Allah ﷻ declares: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ—truly, that is from the matters of great resolve, from deeds of the highest caliber.

The phrase عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ refers to accomplishments that require the highest level of determination and character. To be patient in the face of wrong and to forgive someone who hurt you—this is not small. This is not easy. This is not something you can expect from just anyone.

Don’t expect that just because there’s so much encouragement toward forgiveness in Islam, people should simply be able to do it easily. The people who can accomplish this are high-caliber individuals. That’s precisely why the reward is so immense—because the deed itself is so difficult.

You might encounter someone who is deeply religious. They go for Hajj regularly, they recite Quran daily, they’re known for their piety. But mention one particular person—that cousin, that former friend—and you see an explosion of anger. They cannot let it go. Does this mean they have no faith? No. Allah ﷻ is telling us that forgiveness in situations of deep hurt is among the most challenging accomplishments a believer can achieve.

There’s something linguistically remarkable here. The phrase إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ appears in several places in the Quran, always in the context of patience. In Surah Luqman, in Surah Al-Imran, the Quran describes patience over difficulties as being from عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ. But this ayah is unique. Only here does Allah ﷻ add an extra لَ for emphasis: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ. This grammatical intensifier appears nowhere else with this phrase.

Why this special emphasis? Because every other instance of patience mentioned in the Quran involves situations beyond your control. An earthquake destroys your home. A car accident totals your vehicle. You receive a devastating medical diagnosis. You lose a family member. In all these situations, you have no control over what happened. You only have two options: you can complain and despair, or you can have patience.

But this ayah presents a fundamentally different situation. Here, you have a genuine choice. You can pursue justice, which is your right. Or you can forgive, which is excellence. Both options are open to you. Neither makes you sinful. And yet, even with this legitimate choice available, even when you could righteously pursue retaliation, you still choose patience and forgiveness.

That’s why this instance of patience stands above the others. To choose patience when you have no other dignified option is noble.
