But to choose patience when you have every right to retaliate—when your retaliation would be justified, sanctioned and understood—yet you still restrain yourself and forgive, that represents the pinnacle of spiritual accomplishment.

These linguistic details, embedded in the Arabic structure of the Quran, reveal layers of meaning impossible to capture fully in translation. They demonstrate why the Quran describes itself as قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا—an Arabic Quran. The language itself carries meanings that open new dimensions of understanding.

Ayah 44

وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن وَلِىٍّ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَتَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلَىٰ مَرَدٍّ مِّن سَبِيلٍ

The final ayah of this passage shifts dramatically to the ultimate consequence of persistent wrongdoing. Whoever Allah ﷻ allows to be misguided will find no protective friend after Him. No one can help them. No one can intervene.

Then comes a scene from the Day of Judgment: you will see the wrongdoers, when they witness the punishment, desperately asking, “Is there any path to return? Is there any way back? Is there an exit from this? Some secret passage, some tunnel system that can get us out of this torment?”

The question drips with desperation. These are people who spent their lives ignoring chances to reform, rejecting opportunities to seek forgiveness, dismissing the importance of justice and mercy. Now, faced with the consequences, they search frantically for an escape route that doesn’t exist.

This serves as the sobering conclusion to the passage’s discussion of justice and forgiveness. While we have choices in this life—to forgive or to seek justice, to show patience or to claim our rights—there comes a point when choices end and consequences begin. The wrongdoers will find no way back, no second chance, no protective friend who can shield them from what they’ve earned.

The passage thus completes a full arc: from the beauty of a community that consults and spends in Allah’s cause, through the balanced approach to justice and forgiveness, to the ultimate accountability that awaits us all.

Ayah 44–53 · linked video: 06. Ash-Shuraa (Ayah 44-53)

Surah Ash-Shura: Ayahs 44-53

Ayah 44

وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن وَلِىٍّ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۗ وَتَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَمَّا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ يَقُولُونَ هَلْ إِلَىٰ مَرَدٍّ مِّن سَبِيلٍ

And whoever Allah ﷻ sends astray, for him there is no protector after Him. And you will see the wrongdoers, when they see the punishment, saying, “Is there any way to return?”

Allah ﷻ addresses those who have wronged you directly—those who have hurt you, who have done injustice to you. When someone wrongs us, our natural human response is to want revenge. We want justice. Allah ﷻ is telling us: they’re going to get it. I’m telling you with certainty, they’re going to face their consequences. This reality provides a powerful motivation for patience.

When the wrongdoers finally see the punishment, they will cry out, “Is there any way back? Is there any way to return?” The Arabic phrase هَلْ إِلَىٰ مَرَدٍّ مِّن سَبِيلٍ (hal ila maradin min sabil) captures their desperate plea for any possible escape route, any path back to where they came from. But notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t mention people of help or protection for them immediately after this statement. Why? Because whoever Allah ﷻ misleads will have absolutely no way back whatsoever. They won’t find any path at all.

Ayah 45

وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يُعْرَضُونَ عَلَيْهَا خَـٰشِعِينَ مِنَ ٱلذُّلِّ يَنظُرُونَ مِن طَرْفٍ خَفِىٍّ ۗ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِى عَذَابٍ مُّقِيمٍ

And you will see them being presented to it, humbled from disgrace, looking with stealthy glances. And those who believed will say, “Indeed, the losers are the ones who lost themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection. Unquestionably, the wrongdoers are in an enduring punishment.”

Now you will watch them being presented before the Fire. They will be خَـٰشِعِينَ مِنَ ٱلذُّلِّ (khashi’in min adh-dhull)—full of humility, powerlessness, and weakness. The word khashi’ conveys being in complete awe-based weakness, utterly humbled.

The description continues: يَنظُرُونَ مِن طَرْفٍ خَفِىٍّ (yanzuruna min tarfin khafiyy)—they’re looking with a fearful, hidden glance. The Fire is so terrifying that they want to look at it, yet they desperately want to avoid eye contact. They can’t bear to look at it for too long, but they also can’t help stealing glances. It’s like trying to look at something horrifying—you want to turn away, but something compels you to look back.

At that moment, the believers will declare: إِنَّ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَأَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (inna al-khasirina alladhina khasiru anfusahum wa ahlihim yawma al-qiyamah)—“The true losers are those who put themselves and their families into bankruptcy on the Day of Resurrection.” The concept of loss here is complete and total—they’ve lost themselves and their families, everything that matters.

Allah ﷻ emphasizes: أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِى عَذَابٍ مُّقِيمٍ (ala inna azh-zhalimina fi ’adhabin muqim)—“You had better know: the wrongdoers are going to be in permanent punishment.” The word muqim indicates they will remain in that punishment forever and ever.

Ayah 46

وَمَا كَانَ لَهُم مِّنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ يَنصُرُونَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن سَبِيلٍ

And there will not be for them any allies to aid them other than Allah. And whoever Allah ﷻ sends astray—for him there is no way.

On that Day, they won’t have any helpers to aid them. In this world, when someone wrongs another person, they may have protection, power, and influence. You might want to seek justice, but you can’t because they’re too powerful, too protected. But on the Day of Judgment, none of that will matter. They won’t have any helpers besides Allah ﷻ, and whoever Allah ﷻ has misled will have no way back whatsoever. They won’t find any path at all.

Ayah 47

ٱسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِرَبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَا لَكُم مِّن مَّلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّكِيرٍ

Respond to your Lord before a day comes from Allah of which there is no repelling. No refuge will you have that day, nor will there be for you any denial.

After hearing this description of the Fire and understanding what awaits those who reject faith, Allah ﷻ issues an urgent call: اسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِرَبِّكُم (istajibu li rabbikum)—respond to your Master, O believers. Take this seriously and respond to your Lord.

Respond مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِىَ يَوْمٌ لَّا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ (min qabli an ya’tiya yawmun la maradda lahu min Allah)—before a Day arrives of which there is no canceling, no taking back. It won’t be retreated. Think of floodwaters that won’t recede. When Judgment Day begins, when the angels start descending, there’s no ceasefire, no retreat. There’s no “abort mission” on that Day.

مَا لَكُم مِّن مَّلْجَإٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ (ma lakum min malja’in yawma’idhin)—there won’t be any place you can resort to. The word malja’ means a place of refuge, somewhere to head toward, to find shelter. You won’t find a nook, a cranny, an alleyway, a tunnel, a dungeon—nothing. There will be no hiding place at all.

وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّكِيرٍ (wa ma lakum min nakir)—and you won’t even find anyone to deny on your behalf. The word nakir refers to someone who denies something for you, who stands up for you. Imagine the police dragging someone away, and their family member protests: “He didn’t do anything! What are you doing? Why are you taking him?” That person who defends you, who denies the charges against you—that’s the nakir.

Allah ﷻ is saying: you’re going to be dragged away, and there won’t even be anybody saying, “Hey, wait, don’t take him! He’s with me. He didn’t do anything. He’s innocent.” None of that will happen on Judgment Day.

In this world, wealthy people often have no fear because they can buy protection. They have hiding places—that’s the malja’. They have people who will provide alibis for them—that’s the nakir. Someone who will say, “He was at my house all night; he didn’t commit that crime.” They can buy that kind of protection. But Allah ﷻ is saying: there’s no malja’ for you and no nakir for you on that Day.

Ayah 48

فَإِنْ أَعْرَضُوا۟ فَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَفِيظًا ۖ إِنْ عَلَيْكَ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَإِنَّآ إِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ كَفُورٌ

But if they turn away—then We have not sent you as a guardian over them. Your duty is only to convey. And indeed, when We let man taste mercy from Us, he rejoices in it; but if evil afflicts them because of what their hands have put forth, then indeed, man is ungrateful.

If they still ignore the message, Allah ﷻ reminds the Prophet ﷺ: We haven’t sent you as a حَفِيظًا (hafiz)—a guardian over them. You’re not responsible for them. You can’t force them to do anything, and you can’t protect them from their own selves. As the Arabs say, “You can guard your brother from anyone except himself.” You might want to protect your brother from every problem that comes to him, but you can’t protect him from his own choices, his own mistakes.

Allah ﷻ says: We didn’t send you as a guardian over them. But then He gives us the reason why they’re ignoring the message. Why doesn’t this message resonate with them? Why doesn’t it click?

The answer: وَإِنَّآ إِذَآ أَذَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً فَرِحَ بِهَا (wa inna idha adhaqna al-insana minna rahmatan fariha biha)—when We make the human being taste some mercy that comes especially from Us, he becomes overwhelmed with happiness. He becomes so deeply indulged in those mercies We give him that he forgets about the message entirely. He ignores everything else.

Notice how this ayah begins with one subject and ends with what seems like a different one. It starts by saying they’re ignoring you, and you’re not responsible for them. But then it explains why they’re ignoring you: they’re too involved, too happy with their worldly pleasures. They’re too immersed in their Netflix account, their entertainment, their comfort. They can’t get out of it. When the truth comes knocking, they just turn the volume up and tune it out.

Then Allah ﷻ describes what happens when things go wrong: وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌۢ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَإِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ كَفُورٌ (wa in tusibhum sayyi’atun bima qaddamat aydihim fa inna al-insana kafur)—if any calamity strikes them because of what they’ve done themselves, then the human being becomes extremely ungrateful and disbelieving.

Suddenly they ask: “Why is Allah ﷻ doing this to me? What kind of God wants me to pray to Him when I have all these problems?” I once received a call asking me to speak with a young woman from an extremely wealthy family who had all the luxuries in life but was losing her faith. When I asked what happened, she said something bad had occurred recently. I guessed correctly—something had gone wrong in her privileged life.

What was it? Some girls had been invited to a party, and they decided not to invite her. Her response: “Why is He doing this to me? What kind of God would do that? Why would God torture me like this?”

Here’s the problem: when you become immersed in luxury, not only do you become completely oblivious to the message of the deen—that’s one problem—but you also start feeling entitled. You start feeling like you own these things, like you deserve them. The word for this sense of ownership is حَقّ (haqq)—“This is mine; this belongs to me.”

When you start feeling entitled, believing you own and deserve what you have, then when it’s taken away from you, you start complaining. That act of complaining, when you feel you deserved what you had, is called kufr. It’s different from ordinary ingratitude—it’s a denial of favor. You don’t even acknowledge that what you had wasn’t truly yours. The very fact that you’re complaining indicates that you believed it was yours to begin with.

This is the psychological kufr that Allah ﷻ is addressing. When you say لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (lillahi mulku as-samawati wal-ard)—“To Allah ﷻ belongs the dominion of the heavens and earth”—you’re declaring that you own nothing. But when you say, “This is mine! Why was it taken away? Why did You do this to me?”—it’s as though you’re saying Allah ﷻ doesn’t own everything; you own some things too.

Ayah 49

لِّلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ يَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ إِنَـٰثًا وَيَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ٱلذُّكُورَ

To Allah ﷻ belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. He creates what He wills. He gives to whom He wills female children, and He gives to whom He wills males.

That’s why immediately after discussing entitlement and ingratitude, Allah ﷻ declares: لِّلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (lillahi mulku as-samawati wal-ard)—to Allah ﷻ alone belongs the dominion of everything in the heavens and everything on earth. He creates whatever He wants.

Now Allah ﷻ speaks about one of the great gifts given to people, something they should be happy about. When He discusses His gifts, which gift does He mention first—boys or girls? He mentions daughters first: يَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ إِنَـٰثًا (yahabu liman yasha’u inathan)—“He grants to whomever He wills daughters.” Then He mentions sons: وَيَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ٱلذُّكُورَ (wa yahabu liman yasha’u adh-dhukur)—“And He grants to whomever He wills males.”

Why is this important? Because to Allah ﷻ, that’s the greater gift. He mentions it first to elevate its status. But to us, to human societies—particularly in the culture being addressed—there’s a different attitude. People would be disappointed with daughters and celebrate sons. Allah ﷻ is correcting this mindset, fixing our distorted values.

Ayah 50

أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْرَانًا وَإِنَـٰثًا ۖ وَيَجْعَلُ مَن يَشَآءُ عَقِيمًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ

Or He makes them both males and females, and He renders whom He wills barren. Indeed, He is Knowing and Competent.

أَوْ يُزَوِّجُهُمْ ذُكْرَانًا وَإِنَـٰثًا (aw yuzawwiju­hum dhukranan wa inathan)—or He pairs them up, granting both boys and girls. Some families have both; some have only daughters; some have only sons.

وَيَجْعَلُ مَن يَشَآءُ عَقِيمًا (wa yaj’alu man yasha’u ’aqiman)—and whomever He wills, He makes barren, unable to bear children. The point is clear: you’re not in charge of what you’re going to have. You don’t get to decide whether you’ll have a boy or a girl, whether you’ll have children at all. You’re not entitled to any of it.

إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌ قَدِيرٌ (innahu ’alimun qadir)—certainly He is All-Knowing and All-Powerful. He knows everything and has complete control over all things. These last few ayahs deal with matters of ownership and household—establishing that we don’t control these fundamental aspects of our lives.

Ayah 51

وَمَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُكَلِّمَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا وَحْيًا أَوْ مِن وَرَآئِ حِجَابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِىَ بِإِذْنِهِۦ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ

And it is not for any human being that Allah ﷻ should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a partition or that He sends a messenger to reveal, by His permission, what He wills. Indeed, He is Most High and Wise.

This ayah addresses revelation itself—how Allah ﷻ communicates with human beings. It’s not befitting for any human being to speak directly with Allah ﷻ except through specific means.

The first way: إِلَّا وَحْيًا (illa wahyan)—by means of revelation, inspiration placed directly in the heart.

The second way: أَوْ مِن وَرَآئِ حِجَابٍ (aw min wara’i hijab)—or from behind a barrier. The example of this is Musa (AS) and the burning bush. The hijab was the fire itself—a barrier through which Allah ﷻ spoke to him.

The third way: أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولًا فَيُوحِىَ بِإِذْنِهِۦ مَا يَشَآءُ (aw yursila rasulan fa-yuhi bi-idhnihi ma yasha’u)—or He sends a messenger, meaning an angel like Jibreel (AS), who then reveals and inspires by His permission whatever He wills.

It’s beautiful that this discussion of revelation is mentioned right after the discussion of children, because children are also brought by a form of revelation. The ruh (soul) itself comes into the womb of the mother; the angels come and deliver the soul of the child. So now there’s another form of ruh—the spiritual revelation that comes to prophets.

Why does Allah ﷻ describe Himself as عَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ (’Aliyy Hakim)—Most High, Full of Wisdom—specifically here? Because the ayah is about revelation, and we need to know where revelation comes from. It always comes from above—that’s why ’Aliyy is mentioned. And it’s always full of wisdom—that’s why Hakim is mentioned. Revelation comes from on high, and it’s always characterized by perfect wisdom.

Ayah 52

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ رُوحًا مِّنْ أَمْرِنَا ۚ مَا كُنتَ تَدْرِى مَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ وَلَا ٱلْإِيمَـٰنُ وَلَـٰكِن جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُورًا نَّهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن نَّشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ۚ وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

And thus We have revealed to you a spirit from Our command. You did not know what the Book was, nor what faith was, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants. And indeed, you guide to a straight path.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ رُوحًا مِّنْ أَمْرِنَا (wa kadhalika awhayna ilayka ruhan min amrina)—and that’s exactly how We have revealed to you a ruh from Our command. Some scholars say this ruh refers to Jibreel (AS), the angel who brought revelation. Others say ruh here refers to the Quran itself, since one of the names of the Quran is ruh (spirit).

If you take the second interpretation, you arrive at a remarkable insight: we have a ruh inside of us—our soul—and to give that ruh true life, Allah ﷻ sent another ruh. He sent Jibreel (AS) to carry a package—the Quran, which is also ruh—to our Prophet ﷺ, so this ruh could meet the other ruh. This light could meet the other light.

That’s why the word ruh is so difficult to translate fully. The closest concept to understanding ruh that we have in the Quran is the word nur (light). The origin of the word ruh comes from rawh and rahah, which mean pleasure, contentment, peace, being relaxed. Our ruh was originally in perfect contentment because it was in Allah’s company. We were in direct conversation with Allah ﷻ. This ruh will remain restless and unsettled until it returns to the remembrance of Allah ﷻ: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ (ala bi-dhikri Allahi tatma’innu al-qulub)—“Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah ﷻ hearts are assured” (13:28).

مَا كُنتَ تَدْرِى مَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ وَلَا ٱلْإِيمَـٰنُ (ma kunta tadri ma al-kitabu wa la al-iman)—you had no clue what the Book was, nor what iman was. Even you, O Muhammad ﷺ, didn’t know what true iman was until it was given to you from Allah’s side. We learn here that there’s a dimension of faith that cannot exist without revelation. You simply cannot have complete iman without divine guidance.

وَلَـٰكِن جَعَلْنَـٰهُ نُورًا (walakin ja’alnahu nuran)—however, We have made it a light. First ruh was mentioned, and now nur is mentioned. This beautiful pairing shows the spiritual transformation that occurs through revelation.

نَّهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن نَّشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا (nahdi bihi man nasha’u min ’ibadina)—We guide by means of this light whomever We want from among Our slaves. Guidance ultimately comes from Allah ﷻ.

وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (wa innaka latahdi ila siratin mustaqim)—and certainly you truly guide people to a straight path.

We need to reconcile two apparently contradictory ayahs. This ayah describes the Prophet ﷺ: he guides people to a straight path. But in Surah Al-Qasas (28:56), we read: إِنَّكَ لَا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَن يَشَاءُ (innaka la tahdi man ahbabta wa lakinna Allaha yahdi man yasha’u)—“You cannot guide whom you love, but Allah ﷻ guides whom He wills.”

On one hand, we’re told the Prophet ﷺ doesn’t guide anyone he loves. On the other hand, we’re told he guides people to a straight path. What’s the difference?

The difference is in the Arabic word إِلَىٰ (ila)—“to.” The key is understanding that “guiding someone” is different from “guiding to something.” In English, we have two different constructions: guiding someone versus guiding to something.

Imagine someone asks you, “Where’s the airport?” You respond: “Come out of here, take a right, take another right—you can’t miss it.” You’ve just guided them إِلَىٰ (ila)—to the airport. But you haven’t guided them in the sense of forcing them to go, making the decision for them, or ensuring they arrive. You’ve simply shown them the way.

The Prophet ﷺ is there to guide people to a straight path. He can explain it to us as clearly as possible; he can make the directions crystal clear. But he cannot enforce guidance upon us. Actually guiding us—making us choose the path—is a matter between us and Allah ﷻ.

This is the same surah in which we learned: Allah ﷻ did not send you as a guardian; you can’t grab them by the hand and force them to be guided. But you do a very clear job making the road clear to them. The directions are absolutely clear.

And by the way, there’s never an easier set of directions to give someone than a straight path. The more turns you have to navigate, the more complex the instructions become. But if someone asks, “How do I get to your house?” and you can respond, “The road you’re on? Just stay on it until you see me”—that’s the easiest guidance possible. The imagery itself is beautiful. The easiest path to guide someone to is a straight path.

Ayah 53

صِرَٰطِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَصِيرُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

The path of Allah ﷻ, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Unquestionably, to Allah ﷻ all matters are returned.

صِرَٰطِ ٱللَّهِ (sirat Allah)—the path of Allah ﷻ. This is defined further: ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (alladhi lahu ma fi as-samawati wa ma fi al-ard)—the One to whom belongs everything in the heavens and everything on earth.

The surah concludes by establishing Allah’s complete ownership of all things. Why? Because these final ayahs have been dealing with matters of ownership and household. You’re not in charge of whether you’ll have boys or girls—that’s not your decision. You don’t get to decide when Allah ﷻ will send you mercy or when He’ll take it away—you’re not entitled to it; Allah ﷻ owns it. The Messenger ﷺ is not in charge of revelation—Allah ﷻ sends it however He wants, from behind the hijab, through an angel, however He chooses. You don’t get to guide people—you can only show them what the path is. But the path itself is owned by Allah ﷻ, and its travelers are owned by Allah ﷻ.

صِرَٰطِ ٱللَّهِ (sirat Allah)—it is the path of Allah ﷻ. He owns it. To Him alone belongs whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth.

أَلَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَصِيرُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (ala ila Allahi tasiru al-umur)—and to Allah ﷻ alone, all matters, all issues, all decisions are going to be taken back. The word amr (أمر) also means “decision.” Allah ﷻ is saying that every decision you made will be taken back to Him. On the Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ will have the right to ask us: “Why did you make this decision? Why did you make that decision?”

You know what happens when somebody is asked about their decisions? When someone has to answer for their choices? It means somebody has authority over them. If they don’t have authority, your response would be: “What are you asking me for? I just did it. It was my choice.”

But Allah ﷻ owns all authority. You have no right to say, “Well, it was my choice. I just did what I wanted.” No—to Allah ﷻ all decisions will be taken back for examination, for questioning. This is the ultimate accountability, the final return to the One who owns everything and to whom all authority belongs.

May Allah ﷻ guide us to His straight path and grant us the wisdom to respond to Him before the Day arrives when there will be no returning.
